what: WLSMV for ESEM with ordinal items
title: Ordinal Data: Polychoric Correlations and WLSMV
text: engine = "esem" cor = "polychoric" combined, ackwards() automatically switches lavaan estimator WLSMV (diagonally weighted least squares, mean- variance-adjusted). standard estimator structural equation models categorical ordinal indicators, method used Mplus default. WLSMV genuinely operates polychoric basis: lavaan estimates item thresholds polychoric correlations among ordinal indicators, fits factor model matrix using diagonal weight matrix. combining cor = "polychoric" ESEM engine stacking two separate corrections — polychoric structure precisely WLSMV built model, -level edges computed lavaan’s latent correlation matrix (separately estimated psych polychoric matrix). WLSMV fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) now computed model respects ordinal measurement level. typically look better Pearson-based fit indices data, reflecting better model specification different reference distribution WLSMV uses. See vignette("ackwards-engines"), section “Per-level fit: tells (doesn’t)”, interpret indices bass-ackwards context produce autoplot(= "fit") trajectory chart.
code: x_esem <- ackwards(bfi, k_max = 3, engine = "esem", cor = "polychoric") x_esem #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: esem #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: polychoric #> n: 875 #> k (max): 3 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 23.5% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 32.9% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 39.5% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 5 of 8 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself. tidy(x_esem, what = "fit", format = "wide") #>   level      chi dof p_value       CFI       TLI     RMSEA       SRMR BIC #> 1     2 3616.834 251       0 0.7117569 0.6554864 0.1238665 0.09547997  NA #> 2     3 2448.356 228       0 0.8098533 0.7498069 0.1055573 0.07172502  NA

what: Practical guidance: when to use polychoric
title: Ordinal Data: Polychoric Correlations and WLSMV
text: Polychoric estimation requires fitting bivariate normal model every item pair. p items p(p−1)/2 pairs — 300 25-item scale. Computation usually fast (seconds), scales O(p²) can slow large item pools. Screen skewed sparse scales first. Clinical symptom scales right-skewed items often response category endorsed handful people. can make psych::polychoric() fail default continuity correction (set correct = 0), — many items — drive correlation matrix near-singular, case per-level fit indices (especially CFI, comes back NA) factor scores become unreliable. Run [check_items()][ackwards::check_items] fitting see items degenerate sparse, read “trust result” section ?ackwards warning maps whether report solution. matrix near-singular, ackwards() says records object, print()/summary() keep reminding . [factorability()][ackwards::factorability] companion screen correlation matrix whole: reports KMO measure sampling adequacy (overall per item), Bartlett’s test sphericity, N:p ratio, Ledermann bound many factors p items can identify. Call cor basis plan fit. Read output conventions, verdicts — KMO bands N:p rules thumb contested — low KMO handful low-MSA items useful early signal. ackwards() runs light version screen internally warns consequential extreme (KMO .5, N:p 5, k_max past Ledermann bound EFA/ESEM). note score computation. short answer: yes, can use factor scores ordinal analysis downstream work — caveat scaling, validity. cor = "polychoric", weight matrices stored object derived polychoric R, augment() materializes scores applying weights Pearson-standardized raw data (.standardize(data)). resulting scores calibrated model-implied unit variance rather exactly empirical unit variance, empirical standard deviations close precisely 1. scaling nuance reason augment() warns cor != "pearson"; warning mean scores biased unusable. -level edges tidy(= "edges") unaffected either way — come exact algebra, materialized scores.

(1984). general structural equation model dichotomous, ordered categorical, continuous latent variable indicators. Psychometrika, 49(1), 115–132. Olsson, U. (1979). Maximum likelihood estimation polychoric correlation coefficient. Psychometrika, 44(4), 443–460. Pearson, K. (1900). Mathematical contributions theory evolution. VII. correlation characters quantitatively measurable. Philosophical Transactions Royal Society London, Series , 195, 1–47.

what: k is a decision, not an output
title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: can run ackwards(), need value k_max — maximum depth hierarchy. question “many factors data really ?” single true k hidden data, different selection criteria give different answers depending assumptions optimized . practical question : range k defensible, look? suggest_k() designed help answer . runs five complementary criteria reports recommendations together, can see agree diverge. things keep mind start: k_max maximum depth, claim true structure. Setting k_max = 5 tells ackwards() fit models every level 1 5 examine structure evolves across levels. assert exactly five factors exist. fact, deliberately setting k_max one two levels past consensus — watch factors fragment deeper levels — normal informative part analysis. Overextraction dominant error mode. Simulation studies consistently find common mistake retaining many factors, . overextracted level introduces factors 1–2 strong loadings -level correlations near 1.0 (sign parent factor simply split two without adding interpretive content). Forbes (2023) documents explicitly bass-ackwards context: non-replicable structure tends appear deeper levels overextracted hierarchy. Use criteria identify upper end plausible range, treat levels near ceiling appropriate skepticism. Replicability near ceiling can also measured directly, split-half comparability() — see vignette("ackwards-girard") workflow. criterion decisive . five criteria captures different aspect data, tends err different direction, can fail different circumstances. consensus across multiple criteria trustworthy single recommendation.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: suggest_k() computes five criteria data. logic, typical behavior, practical limitations.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: . Computes PC eigenvalues observed correlation matrix, simulates large number random correlation matrices dimensions. Retains components whose observed eigenvalues exceed 95th percentile simulated distribution. Typical behavior. Tends overextract — often recommends components replicate independent samples, particularly items moderately correlated (usually personality clinical research). Treat recommendation upper bound. useful. Best paired engine = "pca" ackwards(), since operate PC eigenvalue basis. also provides fast, stable baseline across wide range data types. Limitation. 95th-percentile threshold convention, formal test. large samples, even trivial components can exceed threshold.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: . logic PA-PC, applied common-factor eigenvalues rather PC eigenvalues. observed FA eigenvalues compared 95th percentile simulated FA eigenvalues. Typical behavior. conservative PA-PC. FA eigenvalues smaller (exclude unique variance), fewer exceed random-data threshold. PA-FA recommendations typically equal fall PA-PC. useful. model-consistent criterion engine = "efa" engine = "esem" ackwards(): analysis assumes common-factor model, comparing FA eigenvalues FA baseline better-matched test. Limitation. Like PA-PC, can underpowered small samples returns NA observed FA eigenvalue exceeds random threshold (sign data may support factor model ).

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: . extracting k components, computes partial correlations among items (correlations remain removing k components). Reports average squared partial correlation k. Recommends k minimizes average — point components removed much shared variance possible. Typical behavior. Usually conservative — often recommends fewer factors PA. Simulation studies find performs well across range sample sizes factor structures, particularly true number factors small moderate. useful. reliable secondary check catches cases PA overextracts. MAP PA-FA agree, can fairly confident range. Limitation. MAP operates PC components internally (uses psych::vss() fm = "pc"), recommendation reflects component-extraction framework even plan use EFA ESEM engine ackwards().

what: VSS-1 and VSS-2 — Very Simple Structure
title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: . Fits “simple structure” model k: loading matrix item loads one factor (VSS-1) two factors (VSS-2). Reports fit simplified model k. Recommends k maximizes fit. Typical behavior. VSS-1 often peaks early (small k), making conservative. VSS-2 tends peak higher k. sensitive whether true structure actually simple-structure form. useful. cross-check criteria. VSS-1 VSS-2 agree MAP, simple-structure interpretation robust. disagree, data may complex loading structure. Limitation. VSS criteria work poorly items meaningful cross-loadings (common situation personality scales). cases, VSS-1 particular may underestimate k.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: . Generates comparison datasets drawing marginal distributions observed items (preserving item’s shape without assuming multivariate normality). Computes eigenvalues comparison dataset applies sequential one-sided Wilcoxon test (default α = 0.30): factor retained adding significantly reduces RMSE relative previous level; procedure stops first non-significant improvement. recommended k last retained level — necessarily minimum RMSE curve. Typical behavior. Among accurate criteria simulation studies (Ruscio & Roche, 2012), particularly items non-normal distributions — common feature Likert-scale data. conservative PA-PC. useful. Ordinal skewed data normality assumptions underlying PA questionable. CD samples actual marginal distributions, implicitly captures item skewness discreteness. Limitation. Requires EFAtools package (install separately). Needs raw data matrix — run correlation matrix alone. resampling step stochastic, results can vary slightly across runs (use seed reproducibility). suggest_k() reports cd_available = FALSE EFAtools absent skips CD gracefully. note cor = "spearman": four criteria respect cor argument compute eigenvalues requested correlation matrix. CD always uses Pearson correlations internally (EFAtools constraint). cor = "spearman" requested, suggest_k() warns CD criteria may diverge.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: complete reference — argument definitions, return value structure, citations — see ?suggest_k.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: output prints two sections. criteria table shows raw evidence k, using two display conventions: Checkmark / dash (PA-PC, PA-FA, CD): checkmark (✔) marks k criterion retains, recommended ceiling; dash (-) marks levels ceiling, retain. criteria answer yes/“keep level?” question k, number show. Number star (MAP, VSS-1, VSS-2): criteria produce score k — quantity minimize (MAP) maximize (VSS) — raw value printed, single optimal k (min max) starred (*). Reading surrounding numbers tells sharp optimum : lone tall peak decisive, near-flat plateau . recommendations block summarizes criterion’s single suggested k (range PA), consensus range spans minimum maximum across available recommendations. Note block lists six lines default even though suggest_k() runs five criteria: "vss" one entry criteria argument (share single psych::vss() call) reports two numbers, VSS-1 VSS-2, since fits simple structure two different complexities.
code: sk <- suggest_k(bfi, seed = 42) #> ℹ Running parallel analysis (20 iterations, PC + FA)... #> ✔ Running parallel analysis (20 iterations, PC + FA)... [135ms] #>  #> ℹ Running MAP and VSS... #> ✔ Running MAP and VSS... [44ms] #>  #> ℹ Running Comparison Data (CD)... #> ✔ Running Comparison Data (CD)... [5.5s] #>  print(sk) #>  #> ── Factor / Component Count Suggestion (ackwards) ────────────────────────────── #> Variables: 25 #> n: 875 #> Basis: pearson #> Tested k: 1-8 #>  #> ── Criteria (k = 1-8) ── #>  #> k = 1: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0254 VSS-1 0.5178 VSS-2 0.0000 CD ✔ #> k = 2: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0194 VSS-1 0.5839 VSS-2 0.6719 CD ✔ #> k = 3: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0175 VSS-1 0.5913 VSS-2 0.7354 CD ✔ #> k = 4: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0164 VSS-1 0.6215* VSS-2 0.7837 CD ✔ #> k = 5: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0160* VSS-1 0.5738 VSS-2 0.7950* CD ✔ #> k = 6: PA-PC - PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0172 VSS-1 0.5594 VSS-2 0.7629 CD ✔* #> k = 7: PA-PC - PA-FA - MAP 0.0205 VSS-1 0.5613 VSS-2 0.7616 CD - #> k = 8: PA-PC - PA-FA - MAP 0.0236 VSS-1 0.5600 VSS-2 0.7215 CD - #>  #> ── Recommendations ── #>  #> • PA-PC: k <= 5 #> • PA-FA: k <= 6 #> • MAP: k = 5 #> • VSS-1: k = 4 #> • VSS-2: k = 5 #> • CD: k = 6 #> Consensus range: k = 4-6 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: k_max in ackwards() is a maximum depth. Setting k_max one or two levels #> above the consensus to observe factor fragmentation is intentional. #> Caution: PA-PC tends to overextract; structures may not replicate (Forbes, #> 2023). PA-FA and CD are more conservative. Use the range.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: EFAtools installed CD computed, plot 2×2 grid; otherwise falls back single-column three-panel layout. Scree / Parallel Analysis (top-left). solid blue line observed PC eigenvalue profile; grey dashed line PA-PC 95th-percentile threshold. Retain PC-based components blue line grey dashed line (left PA-PC star). solid teal line grey dotted line show FA basis (PA-FA). two comparisons independent — read pair threshold. MAP (top-right). Lower better. criterion minimizes starred k. VSS (bottom-left). Higher better. VSS-1 (solid green) VSS-2 (dashed purple) peak starred k. CD — Comparison Data (bottom-right, available). Shows mean RMSE observed comparison-data eigenvalues k. curve drawn levels actually computed EFAtools::CD (first non-significant improvement plus one). starred k last level retained sequential Wilcoxon test — need visible minimum plotted curve. Requires EFAtools. Look visual convergence across panels. scree elbow, MAP minimum, VSS peak occur roughly k, evidence unusually consistent. scatter, genuine ambiguity, exploring range k values ackwards() right response.

what: Matching the criterion to your engine
title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: best-practice recommendation, hard rule. Running five criteria regardless engine cheap informative — see criteria agree pull different directions.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: default suggest_k() runs five criteria (CD EFAtools installed). criteria argument lets request subset — useful trust certain criteria data, want faster run: Skipping genuine computational saving, just output filtering: "pa_pc" "pa_fa" share single psych::fa.parallel() call (run, neither), "map" "vss" share single psych::vss() call. Requesting "map" alone therefore avoids parallel-analysis simulation altogether. "vss" selects VSS-1 VSS-2 together unit. Criteria request skipped, k_* fields result NA. print() method autoplot() diagnostic render criteria asked , consensus range computed requested criteria .
code: # Just MAP (skips parallel analysis and CD entirely) suggest_k(bfi25, criteria = "map")  # Only the two parallel-analysis criteria suggest_k(bfi25, criteria = c("pa_pc", "pa_fa"))

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: cor controls correlation matrix used compute eigenvalues PA, MAP, VSS. match (approximate) cor argument plan use ackwards(). Ordinal data caveat. items ordinal (e.g., Likert scales), may plan use cor = "polychoric" ackwards(). suggest_k() support cor = "polychoric" — parallel analysis MAP polychoric eigen-decomposition path. standard practice run suggest_k() default cor = "pearson" switch cor = "polychoric" ackwards(). PA-PC PA-FA recommendations Pearson matrix serve reasonable upper lower bound polychoric analysis; see vignette("ackwards-ordinal") .

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: n_iter controls many random matrices simulated parallel analysis. default 20, fast noisy. publication-ready result, increase 100 : quick initial exploration, n_iter = 5 often sufficient:
code: sk_hi <- suggest_k(bfi, n_iter = 100, seed = 42) sk_fast <- suggest_k(bfi, n_iter = 5)

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: seed argument passed set.seed() Comparison Data (CD) step, making CD results reproducible across runs. Honest caveat. Parallel analysis uses psych::fa.parallel() internally, respond reliably set.seed(). PA simulation results vary slightly run run regardless seed argument — known limitation underlying function. CD, uses EFAtools::CD(), respond set.seed() reproducible seed set.

title: Choosing k: How Many Factors?
text: k_max maximum number factors tested. default min(ncol(data) - 1, 8). Increase expect deeper hierarchy; reduce speed computation already strong prior. Note k_max search ceiling suggest_k() need equal k_max ultimately pass ackwards().

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: default diagram reference: Factors labeled m{k}f{j} (level k, factor j). Two edge aesthetics carry -level correlations, one comes legend: arrow thickness encodes magnitude |r|, edge color encodes direction (blue = positive, red–orange = negative). Level labels left count factors per level. Primary-parent edges always positive sign alignment; red (negative) edge therefore genuine secondary relationship, artifact.
code: library(ackwards)  bfi <- na.omit(bfi25) x <- ackwards(bfi, k_max = 5, cor = "polychoric") autoplot(x)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: choose aesthetic carries piece information. sign_by picks channel direction magnitude_by picks channel |r|. aesthetic ever mapped without matching legend.

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: sign_by = "color" (default) uses color_pos/color_neg. "linetype" draws positive edges solid negative edges dashed, freeing color single-hue figure. "" uses color linetype together — negatives get distinct double-dash still read greyscale — merges two single “Direction” legend. "none" drops sign encoding entirely.
code: autoplot(x, sign_by = "linetype") autoplot(x, sign_by = "both")

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: default magnitude_by = "linewidth" maps |r| arrow thickness |r| legend. Set magnitude_by = "none" uniform-width edges (see also edge_linewidth, , pin specific width).
code: autoplot(x, magnitude_by = "none")

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: Edges cut_show hidden entirely. Raising produces sparser diagram emphasises strongest connections.
code: # Default cut_show = 0.3 (already shown above) autoplot(x, cut_show = 0.5)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: default blue/red palette can replaced colors recognised R. British spellings (colour_pos, colour_neg) accepted aliases. sign encoded color (sign_by = "linetype" "none"), edges take single color_edge (default black) — basis Forbes (2023) publication style (see worked example end vignette).
code: autoplot(x, color_pos = "darkorchid", color_neg = "darkorange")

what: mono = TRUE — a black-and-white convenience wrapper
title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: mono = TRUE shorthand sign_by = "linetype" black edges: solid lines positive correlations, dashed lines negative. magnitude_by still applies, linewidth continues encode |r|. mono suits black--white figures reader must distinguish positive negative edges. label edges exact values well, add show_r = TRUE (documented next).
code: autoplot(x, mono = TRUE)

what: show_r / r_digits — annotate edges with r values
title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: show_r = TRUE draws rounded (signed) correlation edge midpoint. r_digits controls number decimal places (default 2).
code: autoplot(x, show_r = TRUE) autoplot(x, show_r = TRUE, r_digits = 1)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: node_labels named character vector mapping factor IDs display strings. Unspecified factors keep name attached set_factor_labels(), falling back m{k}f{j} labels. already labelled object set_factor_labels() (see vignette("ackwards-interpret")), names appear diagram automatically node_labels needed override particular node one plot. Multi-line labels supported via \n:
code: autoplot(x, node_labels = c(   m5f1 = "Neuro.",   m5f2 = "Extra.",   m5f3 = "Consc.",   m5f4 = "Agree.",   m5f5 = "Open." )) autoplot(x, node_labels = c(   m5f1 = "Neuro-\nticism",   m5f2 = "Extra-\nversion" ))

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: Typing every factor ID tedious large objects. label_template() generates full named vector canonical diagram order prints copy-pasteable c(...) literal can edit pass back node_labels. also offers Forbes (2023) letter convention ("A1", "B1", "B2", …) built-style: full naming workflow — reading factors, sign convention, choosing labels across hierarchy — see vignette("ackwards-interpret").

what: primary_only = TRUE — show only primary-parent edges
title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: Setting primary_only = TRUE keeps single strongest edge per factor (primary parent), producing clean tree. skip-level edges never primary, also suppresses curved arcs pairs = "" used.
code: autoplot(x, primary_only = TRUE)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: Level labels (“1 factor”, “2 factors”, …) shown default left margin. can hidden resized.
code: autoplot(x, show_level_labels = FALSE) autoplot(x, show_level_labels = TRUE, level_label_size = 4)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: default, edges end closed arrowheads. Setting show_arrows = FALSE draws plain line ends. applies straight edges curved skip-level arcs.
code: autoplot(x, show_arrows = FALSE)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: default, edge width proportional |r| (via magnitude_by). numeric edge_linewidth draws every edge constant width removes |r| legend — like magnitude_by = "none", width choose.
code: autoplot(x, edge_linewidth = 0.7)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: default levels stack top--bottom (level 1 top). direction = "horizontal" lays left--right (level 1 left), fits wide slides posters; level labels move bottom margin.
code: autoplot(x, direction = "horizontal")

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: legend = FALSE removes legends plot. useful diagram self-explanatory legend duplicates information conveyed labels.
code: autoplot(x, legend = FALSE)

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: following call reproduces visual style Forbes (2023): black lines uniform weight, plain line ends, correlation labels, legend. Setting direction colors black yields single-hue figure, legend = FALSE suppresses now-redundant key. Combining node_labels names factors final figure: pruned-factor variant figure (nodes omitted, spanning arrows) see vignette("ackwards-forbes").
code: autoplot(x,   color_pos      = "black",   color_neg      = "black",   edge_linewidth = 0.6,   show_arrows    = FALSE,   show_r         = TRUE,   legend         = FALSE ) autoplot(x,   color_pos = "black",   color_neg = "black",   edge_linewidth = 0.6,   show_arrows = FALSE,   show_r = TRUE,   legend = FALSE,   node_labels = c(     m5f1 = "Neuro.",     m5f2 = "Extra.",     m5f3 = "Consc.",     m5f4 = "Agree.",     m5f5 = "Open."   ) )

title: Customizing the Hierarchy Diagram
text: autoplot() returns ordinary ggplot object, save ggplot2::ggsave(): ackwards re-export ggsave() — move ggplot2 Suggests Imports — call ggplot2 directly.
code: p <- autoplot(x, direction = "horizontal") ggplot2::ggsave("hierarchy.png", p, width = 9, height = 5, dpi = 300)

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis R ackwards implements Goldberg’s (2006) bass-ackwards method modern extensions mapping hierarchical structure multivariate data. core insight simple: instead asking many factors best describe data, ask solutions different levels resolution related one another. 1-factor solution captures broadest shared variance; 5-factor solution captures narrower, specific dimensions. Bass-ackwards analysis traces broad factors split narrow ones — narrow factors redundant re-combinations broader ones. package supports three extraction engines (PCA, EFA, ESEM) polychoric correlations ordinal data. Beyond fitting, full analysis toolkit: suggest_k() brackets plausible depth, comparability() gates depth split-half replicability, Forbes’s (2023) prune() flags redundant artifactual factors skip-level connections, boot_edges() puts bootstrap confidence intervals every edge, predict() scores new observations sample.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: Install released version CRAN: development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("ackwards") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("jmgirard/ackwards")

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: use bfi25, built-25-item Big Five example dataset (see ?bfi25 provenance). items recorded 6-point ordinal scale, set cor = "polychoric", fit dataset directly (rather na.omit()-ing first) built-IPIP item labels flow top_items().

what: Step 1 — Suggest a range of k
title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: suggest_k() runs five complementary criteria — two forms parallel analysis (PC FA basis), MAP, VSS, optionally Comparison Data — help choose upper bound hierarchy depth. criteria converge consensus range covers k = 5, consistent known Big Five structure instrument.
code: library(ackwards)  sk <- suggest_k(bfi25) sk #>  #> ── Factor / Component Count Suggestion (ackwards) ────────────────────────────── #> Variables: 25 #> n: 1,000 #> Basis: pearson #> Tested k: 1-8 #>  #> ── Criteria (k = 1-8) ── #>  #> k = 1: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0246 VSS-1 0.5121 VSS-2 0.0000 CD ✔ #> k = 2: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0190 VSS-1 0.5761 VSS-2 0.6636 CD ✔ #> k = 3: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0172 VSS-1 0.5969 VSS-2 0.7288 CD ✔ #> k = 4: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0164 VSS-1 0.6198* VSS-2 0.7781 CD ✔ #> k = 5: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0160* VSS-1 0.5730 VSS-2 0.7912* CD ✔ #> k = 6: PA-PC - PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0170 VSS-1 0.5592 VSS-2 0.7530 CD ✔* #> k = 7: PA-PC - PA-FA - MAP 0.0201 VSS-1 0.5698 VSS-2 0.7290 CD - #> k = 8: PA-PC - PA-FA - MAP 0.0231 VSS-1 0.5615 VSS-2 0.7252 CD - #>  #> ── Recommendations ── #>  #> • PA-PC: k <= 5 #> • PA-FA: k <= 6 #> • MAP: k = 5 #> • VSS-1: k = 4 #> • VSS-2: k = 5 #> • CD: k = 6 #> Consensus range: k = 4-6 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: k_max in ackwards() is a maximum depth. Setting k_max one or two levels #> above the consensus to observe factor fragmentation is intentional. #> Caution: PA-PC tends to overextract; structures may not replicate (Forbes, #> 2023). PA-FA and CD are more conservative. Use the range.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: ackwards() fits factor models every level 1 k_max computes -level factor-score correlations define hierarchy.
code: x <- ackwards(bfi25, k_max = 5, cor = "polychoric", missing = "listwise") x #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: polychoric #> n: 875 #> k (max): 5 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 23.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 35.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 44.6% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 52.2% variance #> ✔ k = 5: 5 factors, 58.4% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 14 of 40 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: autoplot() draws hierarchical diagram. row level (k = 1 top, k = 5 bottom); arrows connect narrow factor broad factor inherits , thickness encoding |r| colour encoding sign (legended). visualization vignette covers encodings, direction = "horizontal", rest styling.  five-factor level cleanly splits Big Five. single broad factor k = 1 (roughly general positive character) differentiates first positive vs. negative affect (k = 2), successively narrower traits.
code: autoplot(x)

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: top_items() lists factor’s salient items — printed bfi25’s built-IPIP labels — can read factor means; augment() turns hierarchy factor scores downstream analysis.
code: # What does each of the five factors mean? (salient items, |loading| >= 0.5) top_items(x, level = 5, cut = 0.5) #>  #> ── Salient items by factor (ackwards) ────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Cut: |loading| >= 0.5 #> Top-n: all #>  #> ── Level 5 (5 factors) ── #>  #> m5f1 #> E2: Find it difficult to approach others [-0.752] #> E4: Make friends easily [0.747] #> E1: Don't talk a lot [-0.701] #> E3: Know how to captivate people [0.677] #> E5: Take charge [0.597] #>  #> m5f2 #> N3: Have frequent mood swings [-0.825] #> N1: Get angry easily [-0.810] #> N2: Get irritated easily [-0.805] #> N5: Panic easily [-0.688] #> N4: Often feel blue [-0.646] #>  #> m5f3 #> C2: Continue until everything is perfect [0.735] #> C4: Do things in a half-way manner [-0.716] #> C1: Am exacting in my work [0.690] #> C3: Do things according to a plan [0.679] #> C5: Waste my time [-0.652] #>  #> m5f4 #> A1: Am indifferent to the feelings of others [-0.704] #> A3: Know how to comfort others [0.703] #> A2: Inquire about others' well-being [0.692] #> A5: Make people feel at ease [0.580] #> A4: Love children [0.522] #>  #> m5f5 #> O5: Will not probe deeply into a subject [-0.705] #> O3: Carry the conversation to a higher level [0.655] #> O1: Am full of ideas [0.604] #> O2: Avoid difficult reading material [-0.595] #> O4: Spend time reflecting on things [0.551] #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Loadings reflect primary-parent sign alignment. Use tidy(x, what = "loadings") #> for the full matrix. # Append factor scores for all 15 factors (1+2+3+4+5) to your data # (incomplete rows score as NA -- see ?augment) scored <- augment(x, data = bfi25) ncol(scored) - ncol(bfi25) # 15 new .m{k}f{j} score columns #> [1] 15

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: serious analysis rarely stops one fit. Three verbs turn hierarchy picture defensible result, one scores new data: comparability(bfi25, k_max = 6) gates hierarchy depth split-half replicability — deepest level every factor re-emerges random halves sample (Everett 1983; Goldberg 1990). prune(x, "redundant") flags factors persist across levels without differentiating — Forbes’s (2023) redundancy question. boot_edges(x, bfi25) attaches bootstrap confidence intervals every -level edge. predict(x, newdata) scores observations model never saw, training metric — standard cross-validation pattern. recommended-workflow vignette strings six-step analysis.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: use ackwards research, please cite package: Please also cite relevant method paper(s): Goldberg (2006) https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jrp.2006.01.001 bass-ackwards method , Forbes (2023) https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000546 use extended method (pairs = "", redundancy/artifact pruning).

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: Extracts factor/component solutions levels 1 k, characterises hierarchy computing -level factor-score correlations. "hierarchy" descriptive: edges score correlations, fitted higher-order SEM.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
code: ackwards(   data,   k_max,   engine = "pca",   cor = "pearson",   fm = "minres",   estimator = NULL,   missing = "pairwise",   n_obs = NULL,   align_signs = TRUE,   keep_scores = FALSE,   keep_fits = FALSE,   seed = NULL,   pairs = "adjacent",   cut_show = 0.3,   correct = 0.5,   ... )

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: data data frame numeric matrix observed variables (items columns, observations rows). Alternatively, pre-computed correlation matrix may supplied (square, symmetric, numeric matrix unit diagonal). correlation matrix supplied, engine must "pca" "efa" (ESEM requires raw data), missing cor arguments ignored. See Correlation-matrix input section . k_max Maximum number factors/components extract. Required; use suggest_k() uncertain. Sets depth hierarchy: levels 1 k_max extracted retained. (Note: suggest_k()'s k_max means something related distinct – max number factors/components evaluates recommending depth, depth ; see function's docs.) engine Extraction engine: "pca" (default), "efa", "esem". "esem" uses lavaan::efa() rotation-aware SEs per-level fit indices; recommended clinical/HiTOP workflow (Kim & Eaton, 2015; Forbush et al., 2024). Requires lavaan >= 0.6-13. cor Correlation basis: "pearson" (default), "spearman", "polychoric". PCA/EFA, "polychoric" computes polychoric correlation matrix via psych::polychoric() (requires psych). ESEM, triggers WLSMV estimation via lavaan. cli warning emitted ordinal-looking columns detected cor "polychoric". fm Factor extraction method passed psych::fa(); used engine = "efa". One "minres" (default, robust OLS), "ml" (maximum likelihood, gives chi-square fit converges less reliably deep levels), "pa" (principal axis). Ignored engine = "pca". estimator Estimation method ESEM engine. NULL (default) auto-selects: "WLSMV" cor = "polychoric", "ML" otherwise. Pass explicitly override: "ULSMV" (unweighted WLS), "MLR" (robust ML). cor = "polychoric" estimator = "ML"/"MLR" errors (lavaan support ML/MLR ordered indicators); "WLSMV"/ "ULSMV" continuous cor allowed (valid, atypical, continuous WLS/ADF estimator). Ignored PCA EFA engines. effective value (auto-selection) recorded x$meta$estimator (NA PCA/EFA). missing handle missing item responses. One : "pairwise" (default) – use available observations pairwise. PCA/EFA feeds stats::cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs"). ESEM WLSMV/ULSMV (ordinal), lavaan uses available.cases, computes polychoric thresholds correlations rows contribute pair – MCAR-valid uses full N. ESEM ML/MLR (continuous), lavaan uses listwise deletion internally edges computed pairwise correlation matrix; minor inconsistency documented $meta. warning emitted incomplete rows detected. "listwise" – complete rows used. Reduces data stats::complete.cases() fitting, correlation matrix, engine fit, edges consistent. n_obs result reflects reduced N. "fiml" – Full Information Maximum Likelihood. engine = "esem" (estimator = "ML"/"MLR"), passes missing = "fiml" lavaan::efa() derives edge correlations lavaan's FIML-estimated saturated model. engine = "pca"/"efa" (M38), correlation matrix estimated via psych::corFiml() fed usual W'RW algebra; requires cor = "pearson" (corFiml estimates multivariate-normal matrix) route announced via cli message. Errors WLSMV/ULSMV non-Pearson PCA/EFA basis. Note: FIML improves estimation missingness impute item responses; score materialisation (keep_scores = TRUE) still produces NA rows incomplete observations. See n_obs fit-index sample size PCA/EFA path. n_obs Number observations, (raw-data FIML path) string selecting N feeds fit indices. Correlation-matrix input: positive integer. Required engine = "efa" (psych::fa() needs N chi-square / RMSEA / TLI); optional "pca" (stored NA_integer_ omitted, disabling N-dependent fit statistics). Raw data: N normally taken nrow(data) numeric n_obs ignored (warning). exception missing = "fiml" engine = "pca"/"efa" (M38): psych::corFiml() estimates correlation matrix incomplete rows, n_obs may "total" (default – every row contributing FIML likelihood, matching FIML convention; Enders, 2010) "complete" (complete-case N, conservative lower bound). Point estimates (loadings, edges) depend choice; EFA fit indices , approximate two-step (FIML matrix normal-theory EFA) route regardless N (Zhang & Savalei, 2020). string n_obs accepted path. align_signs Logical; sign-align factors primary-parent lineage? Default TRUE. keep_scores Logical; store factor scores result? Default FALSE (recomputable via augment.ackwards()). TRUE, per-observation scores stored x$scores named list n x k_j matrices, one per level, standardized real score SDs (see augment.ackwards()). keep_fits Logical; store raw engine fit objects? Default FALSE. TRUE, per-level fit objects (psych lavaan) stored x$fits named list indexed level. seed Integer seed stochastic engines (used PCA captured reproducibility metadata). Default NULL. pairs level pairs compute edges : "adjacent" (default, classic Goldberg – consecutive levels) "" (Forbes extension – every pair levels, including skip-level correlations). prune() recomputes -pairs edges demand regardless setting, pruning require pairs = "" . cut_show Edges |r| >= cut_show flagged above_cut tidy() output. Default 0.3. correct Continuity correction passed psych::polychoric() PCA/EFA polychoric path (engine = "pca"/"efa" cor = "polychoric"). Default 0.5 (psych's default), adds value zero cells estimating thresholds. Set correct = 0 psych::polychoric() fails data (error suggests exactly ) – typically happens item near-empty response category items unequal category counts produce sparse cross-cell. Ignored paths: ESEM computes polychoric correlations inside lavaan, Pearson/Spearman bases use . ... Reserved future arguments.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: object class "ackwards". See print.ackwards(), tidy.ackwards(), glance.ackwards(), augment.ackwards() output methods.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: engine = "pca" – original Goldberg (2006) method; fastest; never fails converge; Waller (2007) algebra exact components. rotation = "varimax" – T'=T^-1 property orthogonal rotation enables closed-form W'RW edge algebra keeps within-level factors uncorrelated cross-level edges reflect hierarchical signal. Matches Goldberg (2006), Kim & Eaton (2015), Forbush et al. (2024). Varimax supported rotation; oblique rotation confound -level signal method's core output. cor = "pearson" – silent basis switching. items look ordinal (<= 7 distinct integer values), cli warning suggest cor = "polychoric", available three engines. align_signs = TRUE – unaligned signs make output unreadable. Anchor: m1f1 oriented toward positive manifold; subsequent factor flipped edge primary parent positive. keep_scores = FALSE / keep_fits = FALSE – memory privacy. Scores O(n x Sigmak) often sensitive; raw engine fits can large. recomputable stored r matrix.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: ESEM engine fits separate lavaan model every level 1..k_max. ordinal data (cor = "polychoric", WLSMV) costly sample statistics lavaan derives raw data – thresholds, polychoric correlation matrix, asymptotic weight matrix – depend data, number factors, computed first level reused every deeper level (identical solutions, much less work). matters many items (hundreds), recomputing statistics level dominated run time. per-level model fits mutually independent dispatched future framework future.apply installed. default plan sequential (behaviour change). run levels parallel, set plan calling ackwards(): Parallelism pays per-level fits heavy (large p, several levels); small problems worker startup cost can outweigh . Results reproducible across plans seed supplied. PCA EFA already compute correlation matrix unaffected.
code: future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 4)  # or multicore on Unix   x <- ackwards(items, k_max = 8, engine = "esem", cor = "polychoric")

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: data pre-computed correlation matrix (square, symmetric, unit diagonal), ackwards() runs entirely matrix using W'RW algebra – raw item responses needed. useful published correlation table polychoric matrix computed externally. Constraints behaviour correlation matrix supplied: Engine: "pca" "efa" supported. "esem" requires raw data (lavaan's polychoric computation, WLSMV estimation, per-level fit indices) error clearly. n_obs: required "efa" (psych needs N chi-square / RMSEA / TLI); optional "pca" (stored NA omitted). cor argument: ignored – basis already determined matrix supply. warning emitted set cor explicitly. missing argument: ignored – missingness handled computing matrix. warning emitted set missing explicitly. Factor scores: keep_scores = TRUE error; augment() tidy(= "scores") also error individual-level scores require row-level item responses. $cor field: stored NA_character_; printed "(user-supplied matrix)".

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: ackwards() raises diagnostics fits. fall three tiers mean whether trust report solution: Fatal – fix trusting. result undefined rests broken correlation matrix: constant item (variance) errors – drop (see check_items()). psych::polychoric() fails – usually near-empty response category; set correct = 0 collapse rare categories. level fails converge – hierarchy truncated deepest level ; interpret beyond . near-singular correlation matrix (smallest eigenvalue < 1e-4, recorded meta$near_singular / meta$min_eigenvalue re-surfaced print()/summary()) means per-level fit indices factor scores unreliable loadings/edges rest rank-deficient matrix. (EFA residual-based fallback inflates TLI/RMSEA; ESEM CFI comes back NA.) Trim redundant items, use missing = "listwise", – polychoric basis – collapse sparse categories set correct = 0. Caution – interpret carefully. solution exists may unstable: Heywood case (communality > 1 / negative uniqueness) level – check level's loadings make substantive sense; consider fewer factors. near-constant item (one response category dominates) can drive meaningless factor – inspect check_items(). Ordinal data Pearson basis attenuates correlations – fine quick look suggest_k() screening, report final model cor = "polychoric". Informational – usually fine. Proceed, just aware: pairwise-missing note, merely sparse (rare--present) response category, ordinal-detection warning intend Pearson.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: Goldberg, L. R. (2006). bass-ackwards. Journal Research Personality, 40(4), 347–358. doi:10.1016/j.jrp.2006.01.001 Waller, N. G. (2007). general method computing hierarchical component structures Goldberg's bass-ackwards method. Journal Research Personality, 41(4), 745–752. doi:10.1016/j.jrp.2006.08.005 Forbes, M. K. (2023). Improving hierarchical models individual differences: extension Goldberg's bass-ackward method. Psychological Methods. doi:10.1037/met0000546 Enders, C. K. (2010). Applied Missing Data Analysis. Guilford Press. Zhang, X., & Savalei, V. (2020). Examining effect missing data RMSEA CFI normal theory full-information maximum likelihood. Structural Equation Modeling, 27(2), 219–239. doi:10.1080/10705511.2019.1642111

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
code: # sim16 is continuous with a known 1 -> 2 -> 4 hierarchy, so the default # pearson basis is appropriate. For ordinal items (e.g. bfi25), fit on the # polychoric basis instead -- see `cor = "polychoric"` and the ordinal # vignette. x <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 4) print(x) #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: pearson #> n: 1,000 #> k (max): 4 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 28.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 46.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 57.5% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 67.7% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 9 of 20 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself. tidy(x) #>    from   to level_from level_to            r is_primary above_cut #> 1  m1f1 m2f1          1        2  0.707296113       TRUE      TRUE #> 2  m1f1 m2f2          1        2  0.706917399       TRUE      TRUE #> 3  m2f1 m3f1          2        3 -0.011342395      FALSE     FALSE #> 4  m2f1 m3f2          2        3  0.697092981       TRUE      TRUE #> 5  m2f1 m3f3          2        3  0.716891014       TRUE      TRUE #> 6  m2f2 m3f1          2        3  0.995593231       TRUE      TRUE #> 7  m2f2 m3f2          2        3  0.074615767      FALSE     FALSE #> 8  m2f2 m3f3          2        3 -0.056803218      FALSE     FALSE #> 9  m3f1 m4f1          3        4 -0.016458058      FALSE     FALSE #> 10 m3f1 m4f2          3        4 -0.004812835      FALSE     FALSE #> 11 m3f1 m4f3          3        4  0.663913488       TRUE      TRUE #> 12 m3f1 m4f4          3        4  0.747612767       TRUE      TRUE #> 13 m3f2 m4f1          3        4  0.940647736       TRUE      TRUE #> 14 m3f2 m4f2          3        4  0.031735215      FALSE     FALSE #> 15 m3f2 m4f3          3        4  0.262765620      FALSE     FALSE #> 16 m3f2 m4f4          3        4 -0.212435736      FALSE     FALSE #> 17 m3f3 m4f1          3        4  0.051257892      FALSE     FALSE #> 18 m3f3 m4f2          3        4  0.970778427       TRUE      TRUE #> 19 m3f3 m4f3          3        4 -0.171584098      FALSE     FALSE #> 20 m3f3 m4f4          3        4  0.159752219      FALSE     FALSE glance(x) #>   engine rotation     cor k_max n_obs deepest_converged n_edges CFI TLI RMSEA #> 1    pca  varimax pearson     4  1000                 4      20  NA  NA    NA #>   SRMR BIC #> 1   NA  NA  # Correlation-matrix input (PCA engine; n_obs optional) R <- cor(sim16) x_R <- ackwards(R, k_max = 4) #> ℹ `n_obs` not supplied; stored as `NA`. #> ℹ Fit statistics requiring N (chi-square, RMSEA, TLI) are unavailable. Pass #>   `n_obs = <N>` to enable them. print(x_R) #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: (user-supplied matrix) #> n: NA #> k (max): 4 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 28.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 46.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 57.5% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 67.7% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 9 of 20 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

title: Augment data with factor scores from an ackwards object — augment.ackwards
text: Appends per-observation factor scores every level data frame. Score columns named .m{k}f{j} (e.g., .m1f1, .m2f1, .m2f2), matching factor labels used throughout object.

title: Augment data with factor scores from an ackwards object — augment.ackwards
text: Score computation. Scores S = Z W / sqrt(score_var), Z item z-scores, W per-level weight matrix stored object, sqrt(score_var) standardizes real score standard deviations (Invariant 1: never assume unit variance). PCA method "components"; EFA/ESEM "tenBerge". scaling argument controls means/SDs build Z: default fit-time moments stored object, data score — training data, subset , entirely new observations — lands metric model estimated . Scoring new observations (cross-validation). scoring needs stored weight matrices fit-time moments, can fit ackwards() training split score held-test split without retraining: augment(x, data = test_set) (, equivalently, predict.ackwards()). default scaling = "fit" test observations standardized training means/SDs, "applying trained model" means: test observation's score depend observations happen share split, train test scores directly comparable. scaling = "sample" instead re-standardizes supplied data's moments — deliberate choice scoring sample different population metric, option objects fit correlation matrix (carry raw-data moments). non-Pearson bases (polychoric, Spearman) usual caveat applies either way: weights derive non-Pearson R Z linear standardization, empirical score SDs close exactly 1 (one-time warning says ); train/test comparability scaling = "fit" unaffected. objects fit missing = "fiml" (PCA/EFA), stored moments observed (na.rm) means/SDs training items — correlation matrix FIML-estimated, scoring operates observed responses, observed moments consistent frame; incomplete rows still score NA (scoring impute). Missing data. Score projection applies weights row-wise propagates NAs listwise: observation least one missing item variable produce NA scores every level. differs fitting, uses pairwise-complete correlations. warning issued NA rows detected. Use na.omit(data) scoring NA rows unwanted. Data source. data supplied, scores always recomputed using stored weights – score new observations. data NULL keep_scores = TRUE set fit time, stored scores returned. neither available informative error raised. Scores-output (append = FALSE). default (append = TRUE) scores appended supplied data, following broom convention. Set append = FALSE return score columns – convenient feeding scores straight cor(), lm(), clustering call without dragging item columns along. augment() always preserves row order row count, cbind(data, augment(x, data, append = FALSE)) reproduces appended output exactly. row scores NA (missing item – see Missing data ) still returned place, positional alignment holds even NA scores. rejoin survives filtering scores afterwards, name identifier columns id_cols travel scores.

what: Plot a bass-ackwards diagram or per-level fit index chart — autoplot.ackwards
title: Plot a bass-ackwards diagram or per-level fit index chart — autoplot.ackwards
text: = "hierarchy" (default), renders layered bass-ackwards hierarchy ggplot2 diagram. Factors appear labelled boxes arranged levels (level 1 top, level k bottom default; set direction = "horizontal" left--right layout). -level edges cut_show hidden. Two aesthetics carry edge information, explained legend: sign (positive/negative) shown sign_by (edge colour default) magnitude (|r|) magnitude_by (line thickness default). aesthetic ever mapped without matching legend.

title: Plot a bass-ackwards diagram or per-level fit index chart — autoplot.ackwards
code: # S3 method for class 'ackwards' autoplot(   object,   what = c("hierarchy", "fit"),   sign_by = c("color", "linetype", "both", "none"),   magnitude_by = c("linewidth", "none"),   cut_show = NULL,   color_pos = "#2166AC",   color_neg = "#D6604D",   color_edge = "black",   colour_pos = NULL,   colour_neg = NULL,   colour_edge = NULL,   node_width = 0.8,   node_height = 0.4,   min_sep = 1,   show_skip = NULL,   curvature = 0.2,   color_pruned = "grey80",   colour_pruned = NULL,   show_r = FALSE,   r_digits = 2L,   r_label_size = 2.5,   mono = FALSE,   edge_linewidth = NULL,   cut_strong = NULL,   direction = c("vertical", "horizontal"),   show_level_labels = TRUE,   level_label_size = 3,   node_labels = NULL,   primary_only = FALSE,   drop_pruned = FALSE,   compress_levels = FALSE,   show_arrows = TRUE,   legend = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'ackwards' plot(x, ...)

title: Plot a bass-ackwards diagram or per-level fit index chart — autoplot.ackwards
text: object ackwards object. One "hierarchy" (default) "fit". Controls visualisation produced. parameters ignored = "fit". sign_by edge sign (positive vs negative correlation) encoded. One "color" (default; positive = color_pos, negative = color_neg), "linetype" (positive = solid, negative = dashed), "" (colour linetype, negative drawn distinct double-dash reads clearly greyscale), "none" (sign encoded; edges color_edge solid). Whichever channel used gets "Direction" legend. Colour default reads sign pre-attentively leaves linetype free; switch "linetype" "" greyscale colour-blind-safe figures. magnitude_by edge magnitude (|r|) encoded. One "linewidth" (default; thicker = stronger, |r| legend) "none" (constant width). numeric edge_linewidth also forces constant width value. cut_show Edges |r| < cut_show drawn. Defaults value used object created (x$meta$cut_show, typically 0.3). color_pos, colour_pos Colour positive edges sign_by uses colour. Default "#2166AC" (blue). colour_pos accepted British alias. color_neg, colour_neg Colour negative edges sign_by uses colour. Default "#D6604D" (red). colour_neg accepted British alias. color_edge, colour_edge Single colour edges sign_by use colour ("linetype" "none"). Default "black". colour_edge accepted British alias. node_width Width factor boxes layout units. Default 0.8. node_height Height factor boxes layout units. Default 0.4. min_sep Minimum horizontal separation nodes; passed ba_layout(). Default 1.0. show_skip Whether draw skip-level (non-adjacent) edges. NULL (default) auto-detects: TRUE object run pairs = "", FALSE otherwise. Ignored drop_pruned = TRUE. curvature Curvature skip-level edge arcs. Passed ggplot2::geom_curve(). Positive values curve right; default 0.2. Ignored drop_pruned = TRUE. color_pruned, colour_pruned Fill colour nodes flagged pruned/redundant. Default "grey80". applied object carries pruning annotations (x$prune non-NULL) drop_pruned = FALSE (pruned nodes omitted entirely drop_pruned = TRUE). colour_pruned accepted British alias. show_r Whether label drawn edge correlation coefficient. Default FALSE. TRUE, labels formatted APA style (leading zero stripped, e.g. .23, -.30) placed beside edge using white-background label clears arrowhead. r_digits Number decimal places edge labels show_r = TRUE. Default 2L. r_label_size Font size edge correlation labels show_r = TRUE. Passed ggplot2::geom_label() size. Default 2.5. mono Monochrome convenience wrapper. TRUE, equivalent sign_by = "linetype" black edges (overrides sign_by colour arguments). magnitude_by still applies. Default FALSE. edge_linewidth NULL (default) uses magnitude_by decide edge width. numeric value forces every edge constant width (implying magnitude_by = "none") drops |r| legend. Forbes figures use uniform thin lines (~= 0.5–0.6). cut_strong Deprecated M35 ignored (warning). Edge magnitude now shown magnitude_by (linewidth) sign sign_by; old strong/weak linetype split double-encoded magnitude. Retained existing calls error. direction Layout orientation. "vertical" (default) stacks levels top--bottom (level 1 top); "horizontal" lays left--right (level 1 left), suits wide slides posters. Level-axis labels move bottom margin "horizontal". show_level_labels Whether draw level axis labels ("1 factor", "2 factors", ...) left diagram. Default TRUE. object carries pruning annotations (x$prune non-NULL) drop_pruned = FALSE, level whose factors pruned axis label rendered italic denote status (matching grey node fill); partially-pruned levels keep plain label. level_label_size Font size level axis labels. Default 3. node_labels named character vector mapping factor IDs (e.g. "m5f1") custom display strings (e.g. "General"). Unspecified factors keep label attached set_factor_labels(), falling back default m{k}f{j} ID. Entries override stored factor label node, argument per-call last word persistent labels. warning issued names match factor ID object. Default NULL. primary_only TRUE, primary-parent edges (is_primary == TRUE) drawn. skip-level edges never primary, also suppresses skip arcs. Ignored drop_pruned = TRUE. Default FALSE. drop_pruned TRUE, activates Forbes (2023) pruned-view rendering path: pruned nodes removed diagram entirely retained node connected single strongest kept ancestor straight arrow (even across level gaps). Requires object carry pruning annotations (piped prune()); errors . Overrides show_skip, curvature, primary_only. Default FALSE. compress_levels TRUE drop_pruned = TRUE, closes vertical gaps left pruned levels retained levels evenly spaced; level axis labels (d) still show original level numbers. Ignored drop_pruned = FALSE. Default FALSE. show_arrows FALSE, edges drawn plain line ends instead closed arrowheads (arrow = NULL). Applies straight curved edge layers. Default TRUE. Forbes (2023) figures use plain line ends. legend FALSE, suppresses plot legends (legend.position = "none"). Useful color_pos == color_neg (e.g. "black") remove otherwise redundant Direction key. title: Plot a bass-ackwards diagram or per-level fit index chart — autoplot.ackwards
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   # sim16 has a known 1 -> 2 -> 4 hierarchy (continuous; pearson basis)   x <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5)   autoplot(x)   autoplot(x, color_pos = "steelblue")    # Encode sign by linetype instead of colour, or by both   autoplot(x, sign_by = "linetype")   autoplot(x, sign_by = "both")    # Left-to-right layout (wide slides / posters)   autoplot(x, direction = "horizontal")    # Per-level fit index chart (EFA or ESEM only)   x_efa <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5, engine = "efa")   autoplot(x_efa, what = "fit")    # Monochrome with correlation labels (for greyscale figures)   autoplot(x, mono = TRUE, show_r = TRUE)    # Custom node labels for the 5-factor level   autoplot(x, node_labels = c(m5f1 = "Factor A", m5f2 = "Factor B"))    # Primary links only -- clean hierarchy tree   autoplot(x, primary_only = TRUE)    # Forbes pruned view: omit redundant nodes, straight spanning arrows   xp <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5) |> prune("redundant")   autoplot(xp, drop_pruned = TRUE)   autoplot(xp, drop_pruned = TRUE, show_r = TRUE)   autoplot(xp, drop_pruned = TRUE, compress_levels = TRUE)    # Plain line ends without arrowheads   autoplot(x, show_arrows = FALSE)    # Uniform edge width (no |r| scaling)   autoplot(x, edge_linewidth = 0.5)    # Suppress legend   autoplot(x, legend = FALSE)    # Forbes (2023) publication style: black lines, uniform width, no arrowheads   autoplot(xp,     drop_pruned = TRUE,     color_pos = "black", color_neg = "black",     edge_linewidth = 0.6, show_arrows = FALSE, legend = FALSE   ) } #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.9) flagged 7 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`.  # }

title: Compute a layered layout for a bass-ackwards diagram — ba_layout
text: Top-pass – determines left--right order factors level. factor's ordinal rank |r|-weighted mean parents' ranks level , siblings share parent grouped together. Bottom-pass – assigns actual x coordinates. deepest level (level k) spread evenly; every upper-level factor placed simple mean x primary children: factor one primary child lands directly ; factor two primary children lands exactly halfway . Falls back |r|-weighted mean children factors primary children. Spreading resolves remaining overlaps. passes layout shifted single level-1 node always x = 0.

title: Screen items for problems before factor analysis — check_items
text: Real-world item sets often contain columns break silently degrade factor analysis: item everyone answered way (variance), item dominated one response couple stray answers, item heavy missingness. polychoric basis especially costly – near-empty response category can make psych::polychoric() fail outright (see correct argument ackwards()), near-constant item can produce plausible-looking meaningless factor warning. check_items() reports problems fit, one row per item, can collapse rare categories, drop degenerate items, set cor/correct deliberately rather debugging cryptic error. reports; never changes data. ackwards() runs screen internally: errors constant item warns near-degenerate one, naming offenders.

Required. engine Extraction engine: "pca" (default) "efa". "esem" yet supported (fitting 2 * n_splits lavaan hierarchies needs performance treatment); ESEM workflows, run comparability() engine = "efa" structural screen. cor Correlation basis: "pearson" (default) "spearman". suggest_k(), "polychoric" supported (estimating polychoric matrices every half-sample slow unstable); users analysing ordinal data screen replicability Pearson basis fit final model cor = "polychoric" ackwards(). fm Factor extraction method passed psych::fa(); used engine = "efa". One "minres" (default), "ml", "pa". n_splits Number random split-half replicates. Default 10L (repeated random splits, following Goldberg's practice – single split can mislead luck draw). replicate fits 2 * k_max solutions, default costs 20 hierarchy fits; PCA EFA fast enough typically seconds. seed Integer seed reproducible splits. Can lower k_max asked full-sample fit truncated (non-convergence deep levels); original request kept k_requested. k_requested, n_splits, n_half, engine, cor, fm, n_obs, n_vars, seed Metadata.

Tucker's congruence coefficient (phi) matched half-solution loading columns reported alongside: comparability asks whether two halves' scores agree; phi asks whether loading patterns agree.

what: Screen a dataset for factorability and sampling adequacy — factorability
title: Screen a dataset for factorability and sampling adequacy — factorability
text: extracting hierarchy, worth asking two prior questions: correlation matrix even worth factoring, sample large enough trust answer. factorability() reports standard diagnostics , one object can print pull values : KMO – Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure sampling adequacy, overall per variable (via psych::KMO()). contrasts correlations partial correlations: low value means variables share little common variance variables partialled , factor model little recover. Bartlett's test sphericity – tests whether correlation matrix differs identity (via psych::cortest.bartlett()). Needs N; non-significant result says correlations weak factor. Sample size – N, number variables p, N:p ratio. Ledermann bound – largest number common factors identifiable p variables (hard limit EFA/ESEM, though PCA). Read conventions, verdicts. Every cutoff – Kaiser's KMO bands, 5:1 / 10:1 N:p rules thumb, Bartlett significance .05 – widely repeated rule thumb, settled threshold, contested methodological literature (required N particular depends communalities factor overdetermination far fixed ratio; MacCallum et al. 1999). factorability() deliberately reports numbers conventional bands rather returning pass/fail flag. ackwards() runs screen internally warns genuinely consequential extreme (KMO < 0.5, N:p < 5).

Required N-based diagnostics (Bartlett, N:p) data correlation matrix; reported NA absent.

Read the #> numbers, not a pass/fail.  # From a correlation matrix: supply n_obs for the N-based diagnostics R <- cor(sim16) factorability(R, n_obs = nrow(sim16)) #>  #> ── Factorability screen (ackwards) ───────────────────────────────────────────── #> Basis: pearson #> Observations: 1,000 #> Variables: 16 #> N:p ratio: 62.5:1 #>  #> ── Sampling adequacy ── #>  #> Overall KMO: 0.86 (meritorious) #>  #> Bartlett's test of sphericity: chi-square(120) = 6476.3, p < .001 #>  #> ── Identifiability ── #>  #> Ledermann bound: at most 10 common factors are identifiable from 16 variables #> (EFA/ESEM; PCA is unbounded). #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> KMO bands (Kaiser 1974), the N:p >= 5/10 rules, and Bartlett at .05 are widely #> used *rules of thumb*, not settled thresholds -- required N depends on #> communalities and factor overdetermination (MacCallum et al. 1999). title: Generate a node-label scaffold for autoplot — label_template
text: factor IDs returned left--right, top--bottom order used ba_layout() autoplot.ackwards(), printed literal maps directly onto diagram.

title: Generate a node-label scaffold for autoplot — label_template
text: "id" (default) – every value equals factor ID ("m1f1", "m2f1", ...). round-trip -op: passing result node_labels without editing reproduces default labels exactly. Useful starting point adding substantive labels. "forbes" – values follow Forbes (2023) convention: level-letter + within-level index ("A1", "B1", "B2", ...). Level 1 -> , level 2 -> B, level 3 -> C, . Within-level indices assigned canonical layout order (left right). Requires k_max <= 26 (LETTERS 26 entries); error raised deeper objects. "blank" – values empty strings. Useful starting scaffold want supply every label scratch defaults showing .

title: Generate a node-label scaffold for autoplot — label_template
code: x <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5)  # Start from ID defaults, then fill in your own labels: labs <- label_template(x) #> `label_template()` scaffold (id style): #> c( #>   "m1f1" = "m1f1", #>   "m2f1" = "m2f1", #>   "m2f2" = "m2f2", #>   "m3f1" = "m3f1", #>   "m3f2" = "m3f2", #>   "m3f3" = "m3f3", #>   "m4f1" = "m4f1", #>   "m4f2" = "m4f2", #>   "m4f3" = "m4f3", #>   "m4f4" = "m4f4", #>   "m5f1" = "m5f1", #>   "m5f2" = "m5f2", #>   "m5f3" = "m5f3", #>   "m5f4" = "m5f4", #>   "m5f5" = "m5f5" #> ) labs["m5f1"] <- "My factor name"  # Forbes letter convention: label_template(x, style = "forbes") #> `label_template()` scaffold (forbes style): #> c( #>   "m1f1" = "A1", #>   "m2f1" = "B1", #>   "m2f2" = "B2", #>   "m3f1" = "C1", #>   "m3f2" = "C2", #>   "m3f3" = "C3", #>   "m4f1" = "D1", #>   "m4f2" = "D2", #>   "m4f3" = "D3", #>   "m4f4" = "D4", #>   "m5f1" = "E1", #>   "m5f2" = "E2", #>   "m5f3" = "E3", #>   "m5f4" = "E4", #>   "m5f5" = "E5" #> )

Rows missing items produce NA scores (scoring impute).

what: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
text: prune() never removes anything ackwards object – annotates factors pruned/prune_reason flags x$prune$nodes (flag-, never removes; object keeps every level). separate, cheap step extraction, can re-prune new thresholds without re-running ackwards(): prune() S3 generic (rather plain function) coexists prune generics already defined recursive-partitioning packages (e.g. rpart::prune) regardless package load order.
code: x <- ackwards(bfi25, k_max = 6, engine = "esem")  # expensive   x |> prune("redundant")                            # cheap, repeatable   x |> prune("redundant", redundancy_r = 0.95)        # no re-extraction

title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
code: prune(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'ackwards' prune(   x,   rules = "none",   manual = NULL,   redundancy_r = 0.9,   redundancy_phi = NULL,   min_items = 3L,   orphan_r = 0.5,   ... )

title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
text: x ackwards object. ... Reserved future methods/arguments. rules Character vector controlling auto-rules run. Default "none" (auto rule; combine manual pure manual pruning, call prune(x) arguments clear existing pruning). Options: "redundant" – identify chains factors connected primary-parent links |r| >= redundancy_r (optionally phi > redundancy_phi). Applies Forbes's (2023) retention rule: keep bottom node chain reaches level k_max (specific); keep top node otherwise. Flagged nodes get pruned = TRUE prune_reason = "redundant" x$prune$nodes. "artifact" (alias "artefact", normalized "artifact") – compute Tucker's congruence coefficient (phi) cross-level factor pairs store x$prune$phi, plus structural signals (few_items/orphan/split_merge) x$prune$structural. factors auto-flagged; artifact identification requires judgment (Forbes, 2023; Wicherts et al., 2016). manual Character vector factor labels (e.g. c("m4f3", "m4f4")) flag directly, addition instead auto rule. Standalone manual pruning supported: prune(x, manual = c("m4f3")). Unknown labels error. node already flagged auto rule keeps prune_reason; otherwise-unflagged manual nodes get prune_reason = "manual". redundancy_r Scalar (0, 1]. Adjacent primary-parent |r| threshold redundancy chains. Default 0.9 (Forbes, 2023). redundancy_phi Scalar (0, 1], NULL (default, auto), NA (explicit opt-). NULL: x$engine == "pca" – phi filter. Component scores determinate (exact linear functions data, factor-score indeterminacy), score correlation |r| true correlation components ; phi adds nothing |r| already capture. x$engine "efa" "esem" – automatically set 0.95 (Lorenzo-Seva & ten Berge, 2006). Factor-score indeterminacy -PCA means |r|-alone liberal; conjunctive phi criterion conservative default. cli message announces resolved value. Pass NA disable phi filtering regardless engine. Pass numeric value override engine. min_items Minimum number items factor must primary loader (highest |loading|). Factors fewer min_items primary items flagged few_items = TRUE x$prune$structural. used rules includes "artifact". Default 3L – factor defined one two items -identified frequently extraction artifact rather replicable construct (classic "three-indicator rule"; Forbes, 2023, Fig. 2). orphan_r Threshold orphan structural signal. factor whose maximum adjacent-level |r| (immediately shallower deeper levels) falls orphan_r flagged orphan = TRUE x$prune$structural – connect neighbouring solutions replicate across hierarchy. used rules includes "artifact". Default 0.5 – moderate correlation; factor shares less quarter variance every neighbour structural outlier worth inspecting.

title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
text: x, $prune populated (replacing prior pruning).

title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
text: Forbes, M. K. (2023). Improving hierarchical models individual differences: extension Goldberg's bass-ackward method. Psychological Methods. doi:10.1037/met0000546 Lorenzo-Seva, U., & ten Berge, J. M. F. (2006). Tucker's congruence coefficient meaningful index factor similarity. Methodology, 2(2), 57–64. doi:10.1027/1614-2241.2.2.57

title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
code: # sim16 has a planted redundant chain + overextraction artifact at k = 5, # so the prune rules always have a finding to show (and no ordinal warning). x <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5)  xp <- prune(x, "redundant") #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.9) flagged 7 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. xp$prune$nodes #>      id level pruned prune_reason #> 1  m1f1     1  FALSE         <NA> #> 2  m2f1     2  FALSE         <NA> #> 3  m2f2     2  FALSE         <NA> #> 4  m3f1     3   TRUE    redundant #> 5  m3f2     3   TRUE    redundant #> 6  m3f3     3   TRUE    redundant #> 7  m4f1     4   TRUE    redundant #> 8  m4f2     4   TRUE    redundant #> 9  m4f3     4   TRUE    redundant #> 10 m4f4     4   TRUE    redundant #> 11 m5f1     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 12 m5f2     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 13 m5f3     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 14 m5f4     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 15 m5f5     5  FALSE         <NA>  # Re-prune with a new threshold -- no re-extraction needed prune(x, "redundant", redundancy_r = 0.95) #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.95) flagged 6 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: pearson #> n: 1,000 #> k (max): 5 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 28.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 46.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 57.5% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 67.7% variance #> ✔ k = 5: 5 factors, 70.8% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 13 of 40 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #>  #> ── Pruning ── #>  #> Redundancy (|r| ≥ 0.95): 6 nodes flagged #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: Pruning is interpretive relabeling, not re-estimation. Flagged nodes #> remain in the object; all edges are preserved. Inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #> `tidy(x, what = "nodes")`. #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.  # Manual pruning: standalone, or mixed with an auto rule prune(x, manual = "m4f2") #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: pearson #> n: 1,000 #> k (max): 5 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 28.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 46.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 57.5% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 67.7% variance #> ✔ k = 5: 5 factors, 70.8% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 13 of 40 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #>  #> ── Pruning ── #>  #> Manual: 1 node explicitly flagged (m4f2) #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: Pruning is interpretive relabeling, not re-estimation. Flagged nodes #> remain in the object; all edges are preserved. Inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #> `tidy(x, what = "nodes")`. #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself. prune(x, "redundant", manual = "m4f2") #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.9) flagged 7 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: pearson #> n: 1,000 #> k (max): 5 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 28.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 46.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 57.5% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 67.7% variance #> ✔ k = 5: 5 factors, 70.8% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 13 of 40 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #>  #> ── Pruning ── #>  #> Redundancy (|r| ≥ 0.9): 7 nodes flagged #> Manual: 1 node explicitly flagged (m4f2) #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: Pruning is interpretive relabeling, not re-estimation. Flagged nodes #> remain in the object; all edges are preserved. Inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #> `tidy(x, what = "nodes")`. #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

title: Attach persistent factor labels to an ackwards object — set_factor_labels
text: set_factor_labels() merges labels already stored, can build incrementally. Within single call: normal string sets (overwrites) factor's label; NA "" value removes just factor's label; passing labels = NULL clears labels . scaffold printed label_template() convenient starting point: copy c(...) literal, fill substantive names, pass .

title: Attach persistent factor labels to an ackwards object — set_factor_labels
code: x <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 4, engine = "pca")  # Start from a scaffold, fill in names, attach them: x <- set_factor_labels(x, c(m4f1 = "Alpha", m4f2 = "Beta")) factor_labels(x) #>    m4f1    m4f2  #> "Alpha"  "Beta"   # Merge in more; remove one; everything downstream now shows the labels: x <- set_factor_labels(x, c(m2f1 = "Broad", m4f2 = NA)) summary(x) #>  #> ── Summary: Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: pearson #> n: 1,000 #> k (max): 4 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> k = 1: 1 factor (28.2% cumulative variance) #> m1f1 28.2% eigenvalue 4.51 #>  #> k = 2: 2 factors (46.5% cumulative variance) #> Broad (m2f1) 23.3% eigenvalue 4.51 #> m2f2 23.2% eigenvalue 2.93 #>  #> k = 3: 3 factors (57.5% cumulative variance) #> m3f1 23.0% eigenvalue 4.51 #> m3f2 17.5% eigenvalue 2.93 #> m3f3 16.9% eigenvalue 1.76 #>  #> k = 4: 4 factors (67.7% cumulative variance) #> Alpha (m4f1) 17.2% eigenvalue 4.51 #> m4f2 16.9% eigenvalue 2.93 #> m4f3 16.8% eigenvalue 1.76 #> m4f4 16.8% eigenvalue 1.63 #>  #> ── Lineage (primary parents) ── #>  #> m1f1 → Broad (m2f1), m2f2 #> Broad (m2f1) → m3f2, m3f3 #> m2f2 → m3f1 #> m3f1 → m4f3, m4f4 #> m3f2 → Alpha (m4f1) #> m3f3 → m4f2 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

title: Simulated continuous bass-ackwards teaching example (16 items, known hierarchy) — sim16
text: Simulated; see data-raw/sim16.R full generative model.

title: Simulated continuous bass-ackwards teaching example (16 items, known hierarchy) — sim16
text: Population model. Sigma = Lambda %*% Phi %*% t(Lambda) + Psi, oblique common-factor model 4 true group factors, sampled via Cholesky factorization (base R; MASS dependency): items load 0.75 true factor (cross-loadings). Factor correlations: 0.45 within metatrait (f1-f2, f3-f4), 0.15 metatraits. Uniquenesses 1 - communality (uniform 0.4375 symmetry design ). Ground-truth hierarchy (verified ackwards(engine = "efa")): k=1 recovers single general factor across 16 items; k=2 splits along metatrait line (i1-i8 vs. i9-i16); k=4 recovers 4 true group factors exactly; six suggest_k() recommendations (five criteria – VSS reports complexities 1 2) reach consensus k = 4. Idealized design. planted signal strong clean, six suggest_k() recommendations converge k = 4 – deliberately easy case, building intuition recovering known hierarchy looks like. Real data rarely agree cleanly: bfi25 criteria span k = 4–6. two datasets complementary teaching foils – sim16 "watch method recover structure planted," bfi25 "reason hierarchy criteria disagree." Present sim16's consensus ideal, norm. Deliberate overextraction artifact k=5. population exactly 4 factors, requesting 5th finds real dimension: EFA produces orphan factor zero primary-loading items. prune(x, "artifact") (default min_items = 3, orphan_r = 0.5), factor flagged few_items orphan. true (non- splitting) factors persist essentially unchanged k=3 onward, parent-child score correlations approach 1 flagged prune(x, "redundant") (|r| >= .9 , EFA auto-default, Tucker's phi > .95). textbook overextraction artifact, included Forbes/redundancy examples guaranteed finding teach (unlike bfi25, reliably trigger one). regenerate dataset, run source("data-raw/sim16.R") package root (set.seed(42)).

title: Simulated continuous bass-ackwards teaching example (16 items, known hierarchy) — sim16
